evidence: ExplorationHandlers.java uses Map<String, Object> for every HTTP response object, with 43 total Map<String, Object> occurrences across the API layer (ExplorationHandlers + SketchHandlers). Typed domain models exist (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, Edge) but are converted to untyped maps at the serialization boundary: e.g., formatDiscoveredNoun() returns Map<String, Object> rather than a typed response record.

GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() returns nullable RelationType without Optional<>: 'private static RelationType parseRelationType(String value) { if (value == null || value.isBlank()) return null; ... catch (IllegalArgumentException e) { return null; } }'. The RelationConfig record stores RelationType relationType without any explicit nullability annotation, and SketchHandlers compares rel.relationType() == relationType directly — a NPE risk if a config entry omits the relation_type field.

ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationAlias() maps external API string aliases to internal relation IDs using raw string literals: case 'adj_predicate', 'predicate' -> 'noun_adj_predicates'. If the canonical relation ID changes, this silent mapping breaks without any compile-time signal.

RelationConfig.posGroupFromConstraint() bypasses the PosGroup enum by scanning CQL text with String.contains("xpos=\"jj") etc. The PosGroup enum (NOUN, VERB, ADJ, ADV, OTHER) exists with a fromString() factory, but no fromXposPrefix(String) or fromCqlConstraint(String) factory is provided, so callers are forced into raw string matching instead of enum-typed parsing.
ConcordanceResult is a sealed interface with permits SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult — exemplary use of Java's type system to restrict subtype proliferation.

GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation() returns Optional<RelationConfig>, and all call sites in ExplorationHandlers and SketchHandlers correctly check isEmpty() before calling .get(), demonstrating proper Optional discipline.

strengths: Domain model layer uses Java records with explicit field types (QueryResults.CollocateResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, ExploreOptions) — typed data flows through the core business logic without raw maps.
ConcordanceResult is a sealed interface with permits SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult, correctly exhausting the subtype space and enabling exhaustive pattern matching.
Optional<RelationConfig> return type from GrammarConfigLoader.getRelation() forces callers to acknowledge absence; all API handler sites check isEmpty() before .get().
RelationType and PosGroup enums are defined and used correctly in domain comparisons (rel.relationType() == relationType, switch on collocatePosGroup()).
BlackLabSnippetParser static methods that can return null are consistently null-checked at every call site in BlackLabQueryExecutor.
type_safety.issue_character: Issues are not pervasive through the domain layer but are concentrated at the API serialization boundary, where well-typed domain objects are systematically dissolved into Map<String, Object> with hardcoded string keys — a pattern that covers 43 sites and eliminates compiler-verified field names across the entire HTTP response layer. Isolated nullability and enum-bypass issues in the config/parsing layer add secondary concerns.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase scores solidly in its domain and query layers: records, sealed interfaces, and Optional are applied correctly where they matter most. The significant downward pressure comes from the HTTP response layer, which constitutes a codebase-wide pattern of converting typed domain objects to Map<String, Object> — the Java equivalent of dict[str, Any] where typed alternatives exist, affecting 43 sites across the API surface. Additionally, parseRelationType() returns nullable RelationType without Optional<>, creating a silent nullability gap in the RelationConfig record that callers don't see at the type level. These patterns place the codebase in the 60–70 range: the domain model is solid (pushing above 60), but the API contract layer systematically erases the type discipline the rest of the code demonstrates (keeping it below 70).

issues.summary: HTTP response layer systematically uses Map<String, Object> instead of typed response DTOs, erasing compiler-verified field names at 43 sites
evidence: formatDiscoveredNoun(), formatCoreCollocate(), formatEdge(), formatSeedCollocate(), formatAdjectiveProfile() all return Map<String, Object> with hardcoded string keys, despite typed domain records existing for each (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, Edge, AdjectiveProfile)

buildExploreResponseBody() accumulates response fields via response.put("seed_collocates_count", ...), response.put("discovered_nouns", ...) — any typo in these string literals is a silent runtime bug, not a compile error
43 total Map<String, Object> occurrences in the production API layer
issues.suggestion: Introduce typed response record classes (e.g., ExploreResponse, SketchResponse, ComparisonResponse) for each endpoint's top-level response shape, and typed serialization records for nested structures (DiscoveredNounResponse, CoreCollocateResponse). Use these in handlers and let the JSON serializer (fastjson2 supports records) convert them, eliminating the string-keyed maps. This brings the API contract under compiler verification.

issues.summary: parseRelationType() returns nullable RelationType without Optional<>, storing silent null in RelationConfig record field
evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.parseRelationType() declared as returning RelationType but returns null on missing or invalid value: 'catch (IllegalArgumentException e) { return null; }'
RelationConfig record declares 'RelationType relationType' with no @NonNull annotation — callers cannot tell from the type signature that this field may be null

SketchHandlers.handleRelationsImpl() calls rel.relationType() == relationType in a direct equality comparison — if relationType() is null (from a config entry missing relation_type), this comparison throws NullPointerException
issues.suggestion: Change parseRelationType() return type to Optional<RelationType> and update RelationConfig to hold Optional<RelationType> relationType(), or validate during config loading that relation_type is always present and throw IOException if missing (as is done for id and pattern). The latter is simpler and consistent with the existing validation style in GrammarConfigLoader.

issues.summary: resolveRelationAlias() maps external API strings to internal relation IDs via raw string literals, bypassing compile-time verification
evidence: resolveRelationAlias() switch: case "adj_predicate", "predicate" -> "noun_adj_predicates"; case "subject_of", "subject" -> "noun_verbs" — internal IDs are raw strings duplicated from the JSON config
If a relation ID in relations.json is renamed, this mapping silently fails at runtime (the relation lookup returns empty Optional, yielding a 400 error) rather than failing at compile time
issues.suggestion: Define compile-time constants for canonical relation IDs (e.g., static final String REL_NOUN_ADJ_PREDICATES = "noun_adj_predicates") in GrammarConfigLoader or a dedicated RelationIds class, and reference those constants in resolveRelationAlias(). For stronger guarantees, validate alias mappings against loaded relation IDs at startup.

issues.summary: posGroupFromConstraint() bypasses PosGroup enum by raw string scanning of CQL text instead of using a typed parsing factory
evidence: posGroupFromConstraint(String s) uses s.contains("xpos=\"jj") to detect adjectives, s.contains("xpos=\"nn") for nouns, etc. — 18 string literal checks for 5 POS categories
PosGroup enum exists with fromString() factory but has no fromCqlConstraint(String) or fromXposPrefix(String) factory, leaving callers with no typed alternative to raw string matching
The method correctly returns PosGroup enum values, but the detection logic itself is untyped and fragile: a new xpos prefix (e.g., xpos="NNP") would silently fall through to PosGroup.OTHER
issues.suggestion: Add a PosGroup.fromXposPrefix(String xposValue) static factory that maps known Penn Treebank prefix strings ('JJ', 'NN', 'VB', 'RB', 'IN') to enum values. Then refactor posGroupFromConstraint() to extract the xpos attribute value with a single regex and delegate to this typed factory, centralizing the mapping in the enum itself.

root_causes: The API layer was likely built incrementally with ad-hoc Map<String, Object> response building, and no typed response DTO layer was introduced to mirror the already-typed domain model layer.

Java's nullable reference types are not enforced by the compiler without external annotations (@NonNull/@Nullable) or explicit Optional wrapping, making it easy for nullable return values to slip through without documented contracts.
likely_symptoms: api_map_string_object is the root pattern; the individual formatX() methods are symptom-level manifestations of the same structural gap
